title: Identifying a Triangle by Its Sides and Corners

steps.instruction: Look at the shape. Trace each straight side with your finger.
steps.work: Side 1, Side 2, Side 3. The shape has 3 sides.
steps.explanation: Sides are the straight edges of a shape. We count each one.

steps.instruction: Corners are where two sides meet. Count them.
steps.work: Corner 1, Corner 2, Corner 3. The shape has 3 corners.
steps.explanation: Each place where two sides come together is a corner (also called a vertex).

steps.instruction: A shape with 3 sides and 3 corners is called a...
steps.explanation: The prefix 'tri' means three. A triangle always has 3 straight sides and 3 corners.

title: Telling Apart a Square and a Rectangle

steps.instruction: Both a square and a rectangle have 4 sides and 4 corners.
steps.work: Square: 4 sides, 4 corners. Rectangle: 4 sides, 4 corners.
steps.explanation: Squares and rectangles are alike because they both have 4 sides and 4 corners.

steps.instruction: Look at the sides. Are they all the same length?
steps.work: Square: all 4 sides are the same length. Rectangle: 2 sides are long, 2 sides are short.
steps.explanation: A square has equal sides. A rectangle has 2 longer sides and 2 shorter sides.

steps.instruction: A square is actually a special rectangle!
steps.work: All squares are rectangles, but not all rectangles are squares.
steps.explanation: A square is a rectangle where all sides happen to be the same length.
